      distinctUntilChanged(),      // Don't search if value is the same
      tap(() => this.isLoading = true),
      switchMap(term =>
        this.productService.search(term || '').pipe(
          catchError(() => of([]))
        )
      ),
      tap(() => this.isLoading = false),
      takeUntil(this.destroy$)
    );
  }

  clearSearch(): void {
    this.searchControl.setValue('');
  }

  ngOnDestroy(): void {
    this.destroy$.next();
    this.destroy$.complete();
  }
}

Without debounceTime, a user typing "iphone 14" (8 keystrokes) would make 8 API calls. With it: 1 call. 🎉

⚡ throttleTime() — Only Once Per Time Window

throttleTime(ms) lets the FIRST value through, then ignores everything for ms milliseconds.

"One value per window — take the first, ignore the rest."

Like a bouncer who lets one person in, then waits before letting the next.

import { throttleTime } from 'rxjs/operators';
import { fromEvent } from 'rxjs';

// User scrolls constantly — fire only once every 200ms
fromEvent(window, 'scroll')
  .pipe(throttleTime(200))
  .subscribe(() => {
    this.checkIfNearBottom();
  });

debounceTime vs throttleTime: 

User clicks rapidly: click, click, click, click, click
                     (every 100ms)

debounceTime(300):   ................ EMIT (only after 300ms silence)
throttleTime(300):   EMIT ............. EMIT (first click, then every 300ms)

Use debounceTime when you want to wait for the user to FINISH (search, form input)
Use throttleTime when you want regular updates while something is happening (scroll, resize, mouse move)

🎯 distinctUntilChanged() — Skip Identical Values

This one is simple: don't emit if the value is the same as the last one.

import { distinctUntilChanged } from 'rxjs/operators';

// User clicks in the same input field without changing value
searchControl.valueChanges
  .pipe(
    debounceTime(400),
    distinctUntilChanged()  // Don't search again if value didn't change!
  )
  .subscribe(term => this.search(term));

You can also compare objects with a custom comparator:

this.filterForm.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged((prev, curr) =>
    prev.category === curr.category && prev.maxPrice === curr.maxPrice
  )
)

🕐 interval() — Emit on a Schedule

interval(ms) emits incrementing numbers (0, 1, 2, 3...) every ms milliseconds. It runs forever until you unsubscribe.

import { interval } from 'rxjs';
import { takeUntil, take } from 'rxjs/operators';

// Emit every 1 second: 0, 1, 2, 3...
interval(1000).subscribe(n => console.log(n));

Real Example — Auto-Refresh Dashboard 

@Component({
  selector: 'app-live-dashboard',
  template: `
    

      
🔴 Live Orders

      
Last updated: {{ lastUpdated | date:'HH:mm:ss' }}


      

        #{{ order.id }} — {{ order.status }} — ৳{{ order.total }}
      

    

  `
})
export class LiveDashboardComponent implements OnInit, OnDestroy {

  orders$!: Observable<Order[]>;
  lastUpdated = new Date();

  private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();

  constructor(private orderService: OrderService) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    // Load immediately, then refresh every 30 seconds
    this.orders$ = interval(30000).pipe(
      startWith(0),  // Emit immediately (don't wait for first interval)
      switchMap(() => {
        this.lastUpdated = new Date();
        return this.orderService.getLiveOrders();
      }),
      takeUntil(this.destroy$)
    );
  }

  ngOnDestroy(): void {
    this.destroy$.next();
    this.destroy$.complete();
  }
}

The dashboard refreshes every 30 seconds automatically — no manual button needed!

⏲️ timer() — Emit Once After a Delay

timer(delay) emits once after the specified delay.
timer(delay, interval) emits once after delay, then every interval.

import { timer } from 'rxjs';

// Show a toast notification, then hide it after 3 seconds
this.showToast = true;

timer(3000).pipe(
  takeUntil(this.destroy$)
).subscribe(() => {
  this.showToast = false;
});

Real Example — Auto-dismiss Notifications 

@Component({
  selector: 'app-toast',
  template: `
    

      {{ toast.message }}
      
    

  `
})
export class ToastComponent implements OnInit, OnDestroy {

  toasts: Toast[] = [];
  private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();

  constructor(private toastService: ToastService) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.toastService.toasts$
      .pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$))
      .subscribe(toast => {
        this.toasts.push(toast);

        // Auto-remove after 4 seconds
        timer(4000)
          .pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$))
          .subscribe(() => {
            this.dismiss(toast.id);
          });
      });
  }

  dismiss(id: number): void {
    this.toasts = this.toasts.filter(t => t.id !== id);
  }

  ngOnDestroy(): void {
    this.destroy$.next();
    this.destroy$.complete();
  }
}

📡 fromEvent() — Turn DOM Events into Observables

fromEvent converts any DOM event into an Observable stream!

import { fromEvent } from 'rxjs';
import { debounceTime, map } from 'rxjs/operators';

// Infinite scroll — detect when user is near bottom
const scroll$ = fromEvent(window, 'scroll').pipe(
  throttleTime(200),
  map(() => {
    const scrollTop = window.scrollY;
    const windowHeight = window.innerHeight;
    const docHeight = document.documentElement.scrollHeight;
    return (scrollTop + windowHeight) / docHeight * 100; // % scrolled
  }),
  filter(pct => pct > 80)  // Only when 80% scrolled
);

scroll$.pipe(
  takeUntil(this.destroy$)
).subscribe(() => {
  this.loadMoreItems();
});

🏗️ Full Real-World Example — Infinite Scroll List 

@Component({
  selector: 'app-infinite-list',
  template: `
    

      

        {{ item.title }}
        
{{ item.body }}

      


      

        Loading more... ⏳
      


      

        🏁 You've reached the end!
      

    

  `
})
export class InfiniteListComponent implements OnInit, OnDestroy {

  items: Item[] = [];
  currentPage = 1;
  isLoadingMore = false;
  noMoreItems = false;

  private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();

  constructor(
    private itemService: ItemService,
    private el: ElementRef
  ) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    // Load initial items
    this.loadPage(1);

    // Watch for scroll near bottom
    fromEvent(this.el.nativeElement.querySelector('.scroll-container'), 'scroll')
      .pipe(
        throttleTime(300),
        filter(() => !this.isLoadingMore && !this.noMoreItems),
        filter((event: any) => {
          const el = event.target;
          const nearBottom = el.scrollTop + el.clientHeight >= el.scrollHeight - 200;
          return nearBottom;
        }),
        takeUntil(this.destroy$)
      )
      .subscribe(() => {
        this.currentPage++;
        this.loadPage(this.currentPage);
      });
  }

  private loadPage(page: number): void {
    this.isLoadingMore = true;

    this.itemService.getItems(page).subscribe({
      next: (newItems) => {
        if (newItems.length === 0) {
          this.noMoreItems = true;
        } else {
          this.items = [...this.items, ...newItems];
        }
        this.isLoadingMore = false;
      },
      error: () => {
        this.isLoadingMore = false;
      }
    });
  }

  ngOnDestroy(): void {
    this.destroy$.next();
    this.destroy$.complete();
  }
}

🧠 Quick Reference — When to Use Which

debounceTime(400) — User is typing in a search box. Wait for them to pause before searching.

throttleTime(200) — User is scrolling or resizing. Handle events at most once per 200ms.

distinctUntilChanged() — Don't trigger again if the value hasn't actually changed.

interval(30000) — Refresh dashboard data every 30 seconds.

timer(3000) — Auto-dismiss a notification after 3 seconds.

fromEvent(el, 'scroll') — Convert a DOM scroll event into an Observable.

🧠 Chapter 9 Summary — What You Learned

  • debounceTime(ms) — emits only after the stream is silent for ms milliseconds. Best for search inputs.
  • throttleTime(ms) — emits the first value, then ignores for ms milliseconds. Best for scroll/resize events.
  • distinctUntilChanged() — skips emission if value is same as previous. Essential for search and forms.
  • interval(ms) — emits 0, 1, 2... every ms milliseconds. Best for polling/auto-refresh.
  • timer(ms) — emits once after delay. Best for auto-dismiss notifications.
  • fromEvent(element, event) — turns any DOM event into an Observable. Best for scroll, click, resize.

📚 Coming Up in Chapter 10 — The Final Chapter!

We wrap up the series with the most important real-world patterns every Angular developer should know:

  • The async pipe + loading state pattern
  • The smart/dumb component pattern with Observables
  • The state management pattern using BehaviorSubject
  • Performance tips — avoiding common RxJS pitfalls

See you in the final chapter! 🚀

fullArticle:
Imagine a user typing in a search box. They type "i", "ip", "iph", "ipho", "iphon", "iphone" — 6 keystrokes in 2 seconds.

Do you really want to make 6 API calls? Of course not! You want to wait until they stop typing and then search once.

That's what timing operators solve. They control when and how often values flow through your stream.

debounceTime(ms) waits until there's a pause of ms milliseconds, THEN lets the latest value through.

Think of it like this: "Ignore everything until they stop for a moment."

Like a person who waits for you to finish talking before responding.

Without debounceTime, a user typing "iphone 14" (8 keystrokes) would make 8 API calls. With it: 1 call. 🎉

title: The World's Largest Cities in 2026 by Population
summary: Con motivo del Día Mundial de la Población, la ONU centra su campaña 2026 en la relación entre las decisiones de los jóvenes y las ciudades que habitan. El artículo presenta el ranking de las 20 urbes más pobladas del planeta según estimaciones de World Population Review, destacando la presión sobre infraestructuras y la necesidad de planificación urbana inclusiva.
imageAlt: The World's Largest Cities in 2026 by Population
aiSummary: Con motivo del Día Mundial de la Población, la ONU centra su campaña 2026 en la relación entre las decisiones de los jóvenes y las ciudades que habitan. El artículo presenta el ranking de las 20 urbes más pobladas del planeta según estimaciones de World Population Review, destacando la presión sobre infraestructuras y la necesidad de planificación urbana inclusiva.
keyPoints: La campaña de la ONU para el Día Mundial de la Población 2026 destaca que las ciudades moldean las decisiones de educación, empleo y vida familiar de los jóvenes globales.
Más de la mitad de la población mundial vive en zonas urbanas, proporción que alcanzará el 70% en 2050, generando presión sobre vivienda e infraestructuras.
El ranking de WPR incluye las 20 ciudades más pobladas de 2026, con datos de crecimiento anual, aunque sus cifras no son directamente comparables por diferencias en la definición de área urbana.
La rápida expansión metropolitana refuerza la necesidad de políticas de planificación urbana inclusivas y resilientes para adaptarse al cambio climático y la demanda de servicios.
whyMatters: Este ranking no es solo un ejercicio demográfico: evidencia la presión sin precedentes que sufren las zonas urbanas para ofrecer vivienda asequible, transporte eficiente y espacios públicos de calidad, un reto que afecta de lleno a ciudades españolas como Madrid y Barcelona, que experimentan crecimientos poblacionales sostenidos. Las decisiones de planificación que se tomen en los próximos años definirán si estas urbes pueden ofrecer oportunidades a las generaciones jóvenes, tal como destaca la campaña de la ONU, o si se profundizarán las desigualdades espaciales y sociales. Además, la concentración poblacional en áreas metropolitanas incrementa la vulnerabilidad climática, por lo que las estrategias de adaptación que se implementen en las ciudades europeas servirán de referencia para el resto del mundo en la lucha contra el cambio climático.

multimedia.alt: The World's Largest Cities in 2026 by Population
multimedia.caption: Imagen de portada

fullArticle:
Con motivo del Día Mundial de la Población, que se celebra cada 11 de julio, la Organización de las Naciones Unidas ha centrado su campaña de 2026 en el lema 'Realizar las esperanzas y aspiraciones de los jóvenes: hoy y para el futuro', poniendo el foco en la estrecha relación entre las decisiones que toman los jóvenes en materia de educación, empleo, vivienda y vida familiar y las características de las ciudades en las que residen. Esta iniciativa se apoya en los resultados de la encuesta global Lives, Choices and Futures, que recogió más de 108.000 respuestas de jóvenes adultos de 73 países, y que evidencia cómo el entorno urbano condiciona de forma creciente las oportunidades disponibles para este grupo poblacional.

Más de la mitad de la población mundial reside actualmente en zonas urbanas, una proporción que se prevé que alcance el 70% en 2050, lo que convierte a las ciudades en el epicentro de la transformación demográfica global. Este crecimiento sostenido genera una presión creciente sobre la vivienda, las infraestructuras, las redes de movilidad, los espacios públicos y los sistemas de adaptación climática, al tiempo que refuerza la importancia de la arquitectura, la planificación urbanística y las políticas públicas para construir entornos urbanos más inclusivos y resilientes. Las tendencias poblacionales permiten identificar las regiones metropolitanas donde estas presiones se concentran de forma más intensa, ofreciendo una visión clara de las urbes que experimentan un crecimiento más acelerado.

A continuación se presenta el ranking de las 20 ciudades más pobladas del mundo en 2026, elaborado a partir de las últimas estimaciones publicadas por World Population Review (WPR), que incluye el crecimiento poblacional anual de cada urbe respecto al año anterior. Es importante tener en cuenta que esta clasificación se basa en estimaciones propias de WPR, y que las diferencias en la definición de 'ciudad propiamente dicha', 'área metropolitana' y 'aglomeración urbana' entre los distintos países hacen que las cifras no sean directamente comparables en todos los casos: urbes con grandes poblaciones suburbanas, como Tokio o Yakarta, pueden aparecer en posiciones más bajas del ranking que cuando se utilizan definiciones más amplias de área metropolitana.

Images shared on social media showed dozens of people stood in Edgware Road, near Hyde Park, and some throwing bottles and other objects at police.

Police said the trouble started after people blocked traffic in the road.

"The incident then escalated with the group throwing bottles and setting off fireworks," a spokesman said. "One officer was taken to hospital for head injuries, it is believed he had been hit by a glass bottle."

multimedia.caption: Imagen de portada

fullArticle:
The vuvuzelas fell quiet in Morocco on Thursday as the only African team left in the World Cup came up against the insurmountable hurdle of France and Kylian Mbappe.

The team that had shown such swashbuckling football in the previous rounds produced an under-par performance as they fell 2-0 to the French in the quarter-final in Foxborough, Massachusetts, said AFP.

Azirar, 18, was still reeling from the defeat after watching the game in Rabat.

"It was a really difficult match for Morocco," he said, although he had to admit that "France deserved to win."

Fans' hopes were sky-high for the 2026 tournament after Morocco stunned the footballing world by reaching the semi-finals four years ago in Qatar, led by world-class captain Achraf Hakimi of Paris Saint-Germain.

Having added outstanding...

Spain's defence has yet to be breached this summer Alfredo Estrella/AFP via Getty Images

One major byproduct of FIFA’s expansion to a 48-team World Cup is that even the best sides can slip a little under the radar.

Spain haven’t been spoken about too much since their shock goalless draw to Cape Verde in their opening game. But suddenly, they find themselves in the last eight and look to be in ominous form, the only nation yet to concede a single goal. In fact, Spain have got the record for the longest time without conceding a World Cup goal, with it being 609 minutes played since they conceded to Japan in Qatar in 2022.

Their route through to a quarter-final clash with Belgium hasn’t had the thrills and spills of some of the other contenders, but the clinical efficiency of Luis de la Fu...

title: Dos proyectos con LangGraph que me enseñaron a diseñar sistemas multiagente: lecciones para desarrolladores
summary: Un profesional con 15 años de experiencia en gestión de equipos y DevOps comparte las lecciones aprendidas al construir dos sistemas multiagente con LangGraph para automatizar la generación de notas de versión y la optimización de pipelines de CI/CD, destacando patrones de diseño clave para la interacción humano-agente.
imageAlt: Dos proyectos con LangGraph que me enseñaron a diseñar sistemas multiagente: lecciones para desarrolladores
aiSummary: Un profesional con 15 años de experiencia en gestión de equipos y DevOps comparte las lecciones aprendidas al construir dos sistemas multiagente con LangGraph para automatizar la generación de notas de versión y la optimización de pipelines de CI/CD, destacando patrones de diseño clave para la interacción humano-agente.
keyPoints: LangGraph se adapta a profesionales de DevOps al estructurar los agentes como DAGs con ramas condicionales, etapas paralelas y puertas de aprobación, similares a pipelines de CI.
El primer proyecto automatiza notas de versión con un supervisor que enruta commits a subagentes, con interrupciones humanas diferenciadas para cambios disruptivos y parches de seguridad.
El segundo proyecto optimiza pipelines de CI/CD de gran escala, identificando cuellos de botella y proponiendo ajustes con estimaciones de ahorro de tiempo y coste.
El diseño de interrupciones humanas adaptadas al tipo de juicio y un bucle de refinamiento cíclico evitan la fatiga por validaciones excesivas y errores de revisión.
whyMatters: Estos patrones de diseño de sistemas multiagente suponen un avance clave para la adopción de la inteligencia artificial en entornos profesionales de desarrollo, al reducir la carga de trabajo repetitivo de los equipos de DevOps sin generar fricción en sus flujos de trabajo habituales. La correcta gestión de las interrupciones humanas adaptadas al tipo de decisión requerida resuelve uno de los principales obstáculos para la confianza en herramientas de IA: la saturación por validaciones innecesarias o el riesgo de pasar por alto errores críticos. A largo plazo, estos enfoques sientan las bases para sistemas de agente escalables que se integren de forma natural en procesos empresariales, acelerando la entrega de software y reduciendo los costes operativos de los equipos de desarrollo.

multimedia.alt: Imagen de portada del artículo sobre dos proyectos con LangGraph para diseñar sistemas multiagente
multimedia.caption: Portada del artículo original publicado en Dev.to que detalla las lecciones aprendidas en el desarrollo de sistemas multiagente con LangGraph

fullArticle:
Con 15 años de experiencia en puestos de gestión de equipos y DevOps, el autor del artículo original en Dev.to decidió volver a la programación de la mano de la inteligencia artificial, con el objetivo de no solo usar herramientas existentes, sino construir sus propios agentes para automatizar tareas repetitivas de su día a día. LangGraph se convirtió en su herramienta favorita para esta tarea, ya que su estructura se alinea perfectamente con los conceptos que ya domina: grafos acíclicos dirigidos (DAGs), ramas condicionales, etapas paralelas y puertas de aprobación, funcionando básicamente como un pipeline de CI en el que algunos nodos ejecutan llamadas a un modelo de lenguaje grande (LLM) en lugar de scripts de shell. Para validar su enfoque, desarrolló dos proyectos consecutivos que resolvían problemas muy diferentes pero compartían patrones de diseño comunes.

[MULTIMEDIA: https://media2.dev.to/dynamic/image/width=1200,height=627,fit=cover,gravity=auto,format=auto/https%3A%2F%2Fdev-to-uploads.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com%2Fuploads%2Farticles%2Fk1kko52oaray2tvy3t4g.png]

El primer proyecto aborda la tediosa tarea de redactar notas de versión, que requiere revisar cientos de commits, filtrar los relevantes, agruparlos por tipo y redactar un texto coherente, además de decidir el tipo de versión (mayor, menor o parche). El agente desarrollado automatiza la parte mecánica del proceso: un supervisor enruta cada commit a un subagente especializado según el tipo de cambio, los cambios disruptivos se detienen para revisión humana individual (cada uno requiere consejos de migración específicos), los parches de seguridad se revisan en bloque al tener un formato estandarizado, y la etapa de redacción consulta notas de versión anteriores del mismo repositorio para imitar el estilo de redacción. El aprendizaje clave de este proyecto es la combinación de un supervisor enrutador con interrupciones humanas híbridas, adaptadas al tipo de juicio que requiere cada paso, para no saturar a los equipos con validaciones innecesarias.

El segundo proyecto resuelve una frustración común en repositorios de gran tamaño: los pipelines de CI que tardan entre 30 y 120 minutos, en los que nadie audita manualmente los archivos YAML de configuración para detectar ineficiencias como cachés faltantes, pasos serializados que podrían ejecutarse en paralelo o contextos de construcción sobredimensionados. El agente desarrollado descarga los archivos de flujo de trabajo y el historial de ejecuciones, perfila cada trabajo, clasifica los cuellos de botella y propone ajustes con estimaciones de ahorro de tiempo y coste, deteniéndose solo para que el equipo humano decida entre las opciones (por ejemplo, 'activar la caché ahorra 5 minutos pero cuesta 0,02 dólares por ejecución'). El patrón clave aquí es el bucle de refinamiento cíclico: proponer, simular, refinar y mostrar al humano, con un único punto de validación en lugar de múltiples interrupciones. Ambos proyectos comparten además un sistema de manejo de respuestas inválidas de LLM: si el modelo devuelve JSON inválido o contenido vacío, se reintenta con un prompt más estricto, y si falla de nuevo, se aplican reglas predefinidas para que el pipeline no se bloquee.

title: ¡ALERTA F1! Bahréin DESAFÍA las tensiones geopolíticas para RESCATAR su Gran Premio de 2026
summary: El gobierno de Bahréin mantiene la esperanza de reprogramar su Gran Premio de Fórmula 1 de 2026, cancelado previamente, para el 4 de octubre, a pesar de los importantes obstáculos logísticos y el recrudecimiento de las tensiones en Oriente Medio. La situación geopolítica, con el resurgimiento de ataques entre EE. UU. e Irán, amenaza no solo esta carrera sino también los eventos finales de la temporada en Qatar y Abu Dabi, lo que obliga a la F1 a considerar opciones alternativas.
imageAlt: ¡ALERTA F1! Bahréin DESAFÍA las tensiones geopolíticas para RESCATAR su Gran Premio de 2026
aiSummary: El gobierno de Bahréin mantiene la esperanza de reprogramar su Gran Premio de Fórmula 1 de 2026, cancelado previamente, para el 4 de octubre, a pesar de los importantes obstáculos logísticos y el recrudecimiento de las tensiones en Oriente Medio. La situación geopolítica, con el resurgimiento de ataques entre EE. UU. e Irán, amenaza no solo esta carrera sino también los eventos finales de la temporada en Qatar y Abu Dabi, lo que obliga a la F1 a considerar opciones alternativas.
keyPoints: Bahréin insiste en celebrar su Gran Premio de F1 de 2026 el 4 de octubre, buscando reinsertar la carrera cancelada en un calendario ya saturado que finalizará la primera semana de diciembre.

La Fórmula 1 enfrenta desafíos logísticos considerables, incluyendo la protección del descanso de verano de los equipos y la dificultad de encajar la carrera sin generar una secuencia excesiva de eventos consecutivos.

Las esperanzas de Bahréin se ven seriamente comprometidas por el deterioro de la situación en Oriente Medio, con el reinicio de los ataques entre EE. UU. e Irán, afectando la navegación en el Estrecho de Ormuz.

Una escalada de las tensiones geopolíticas en la región podría poner en riesgo las dos últimas carreras de la temporada 2026, programadas en Qatar y Abu Dabi, para las cuales la F1 ya ha previsto 'opciones diferentes'.
whyMatters: La persistencia de Bahréin por celebrar su Gran Premio de Fórmula 1 en medio de un calendario ya apretado y una escalada de tensiones en Oriente Medio subraya la compleja interconexión entre el deporte de élite y la geopolítica global. Este escenario no solo plantea desafíos logísticos y financieros sin precedentes para la F1 y sus equipos, sino que también pone de manifiesto la vulnerabilidad de eventos internacionales ante conflictos regionales. Para el lector, esta situación es crucial porque un posible ajuste o cancelación definitiva no solo alteraría el emocionante desenlace de la temporada, sino que también podría sentar un precedente sobre cómo los grandes eventos deportivos gestionan los riesgos geopolíticos en el futuro, afectando directamente la seguridad y la viabilidad de otras competiciones en zonas de conflicto.

multimedia.alt: ¡ALERTA F1! Bahréin DESAFÍA las tensiones geopolíticas para RESCATAR su Gran Premio de 2026
multimedia.caption: Imagen de portada

fullArticle:
El gobierno de Bahréin mantiene viva la esperanza de que su ronda del Campeonato Mundial de Fórmula 1, previamente cancelada para 2026, pueda celebrarse antes de que termine la temporada. La ministra de Desarrollo Sostenible de Bahréin, Noor bint Ali Alkhulaif, ha declarado que existe la posibilidad de que la carrera se reprograme para el 4 de octubre. Esta fecha crearía un intenso triplete de carreras consecutivas, precedida por el Gran Premio de Azerbaiyán y seguida por el evento en Singapur, un desafío logístico considerable para los equipos y la organización.

Sin embargo, la Fórmula 1 enfrenta obstáculos significativos para reinsertar cualquier carrera en un calendario ya de 24 rondas, que debe concluir la primera semana de diciembre. Las pocas semanas libres disponibles no pueden ocuparse sin infringir las vacaciones de verano de los equipos, protegidas por las regulaciones de la F1, o sin generar una serie de carreras consecutivas que implicarían un costoso quebradero de cabeza logístico. Las esperanzas de Bahréin se han visto aún más mermadas esta semana debido al deterioro de la situación en Oriente Medio. Estados Unidos e Irán, que el mes pasado acordaron un Memorándum de Entendimiento para cesar las hostilidades, han intercambiado ataques nuevamente en los últimos días, provocando un descenso en el tráfico marítimo en el vital Estrecho de Ormuz. Una mayor escalada podría incluso poner en peligro las dos últimas carreras de la temporada 2026, programadas en Qatar y Abu Dabi, para las cuales el CEO de la Fórmula 1, Stefano Domenicali, ya ha afirmado tener 'opciones diferentes' en caso de que no puedan llevarse a cabo.

title: A Browser Extension for Researchers/Learners... - Part 1
summary:
As someone who loves learning, I spend a lot of time researching different topics every day. I jump between Google AI Mode, ChatGPT, Claude, blogs, documentation, eBooks, research papers, etc.

The problem is that when I come back to the same topic a day or two later, I realize I've forgotten most of what I learned. The information is scattered across multiple tabs and sources, and while taking notes would help, I'm honestly not consistent enough to do it every time.

There are already tools that summarize web pages, but they usually summarize a single page or require you to manually select the content you want. That wasn't quite what I was looking for.

So I started building a browser extension that captures and summarizes an entire research session instead of individual pages.

The workflow is simple:

Step 1: Capture

  • Turn on a capture toggle before you start researching.
  • Choose which browser tabs should be included in the session.
  • As you browse, the extension captures the content from each selected page.

Step 2: Summarize

  • As each page is captured, it is immediately summarized instead of waiting until the end of the session.
  • This process repeats for every page you visit, creating a concise summary for each source. For the MVP, I'm using the on-device Gemini Nano model to do the summarization.
  • Finally, you can export all the summaries as a single text file or PDF for future reference.

Right now, I'm focusing on building the MVP. I plan to use it myself for two weeks to see whether it actually improves my own learning workflow. If it proves useful, I'll continue improving it and run a small beta with around 10 users to gather feedback before taking it further.

This is one of those projects that started because I wanted to solve my own problem first. Hopefully, it ends up helping other researchers, students, developers, and lifelong learners as well.

I'll keep sharing updates as the project evolves.

Building products that solve your own problems is one of the most rewarding parts of being a developer.

Happy coding everyone! ( •̀ ω •́ )✧

title: The Cloud Readiness Checklist: 6 checks before scale gets expensive
summary:
Cloud problems rarely start as one obvious failure.

-> Sometimes the bill improves, but nobody can explain which product benefited.

-> Sometimes GPUs are reserved, but the workload is not ready for the window.

-> Sometimes caching makes the product faster, but nobody owns refresh.

-> Sometimes the provider looks available, but users in one region still feel slow paths.

These are not separate problems. They point to the same operating question:

Is the cloud setup ready for customer usage, cost pressure, and changing product needs?

That is what this checklist is for.

It is not a replacement for cloud architecture reviews, incident response plans, FinOps tooling, or provider documentation. It is a practical field checklist for SaaS teams that need to connect cloud decisions to product outcomes.

Why cloud readiness needs more than uptime

Cloud readiness is often discussed through infrastructure terms: regions, instances, reservations, cache layers, monitoring, and networking.

Those terms matter.

But founders and product teams usually need a different view:

  • Can customers complete the workflow?
  • Can we explain the bill?
  • Can we reuse work safely?
  • Can we use reserved capacity well?
  • Can we see regional impact?
  • Can we respond when the preferred path slows down?

A cloud system can look technically sound while still creating cost confusion, slow customer paths, stale responses, or capacity waste.

That is why a readiness checklist should cover both infrastructure and product consequences.

The 6-check cloud readiness framework

1. Cost attribution

Cloud savings are useful only when the company can explain who benefited.

Shared discounts, committed use discounts, savings plans, credits, reservations, and platform-level infrastructure can reduce the total bill. But if those savings are pooled across projects, products, or workloads, the reporting model needs to stay clear.

A SaaS team should be able to answer:

  • Which workload created the usage?
  • Which product received the discount benefit?
  • Which project absorbed uncovered usage?
  • Which team owns renewal or adjustment?
  • Does the dashboard show allocated savings, or only total spend?

This matters because product decisions depend on interpretable numbers.

If an AI workload appears cheaper, the team needs to know whether it became more efficient or was covered by shared discount capacity.

If a product line looks profitable, the team needs to know whether infrastructure costs are being allocated in a way that matches usage.

Readiness check:
Cloud savings need attribution, not only a lower total.

2. Capacity timing

Reserved capacity can be valuable when work is prepared for the window.

It can become expensive when the team books capacity before the workload is ready.

This matters most for AI and ML workloads, batch processing, evaluation runs, high-volume migrations, and compute-heavy jobs that need specific capacity during a specific period.

Before reserving capacity, check:

  • What job is the capacity supporting?
  • When does the work need to run?
  • What must be ready before the window starts?
  • What utilization would justify the spend?
  • What happens if the workload slips?
  • Who owns the capacity decision?
  • How will success be measured?

The question is not only whether the compute is affordable.

The question is whether the work can use the window well.

Readiness check:
Capacity planning should follow workload readiness, not only pricing pressure.

3. Cache freshness

Caching can reduce latency and repeated compute work.

But caching also creates a freshness question.

A response that is safe to reuse can improve performance and cost. A response that should have changed can damage trust.

Before caching a product path, define:

  • What response is being cached?
  • Who sees that response?
  • What makes it change?
  • How long can it be reused?
  • Who owns purge or refresh?
  • What should never be cached?
  • What will be measured besides speed?

Public documentation pages may be safe to cache. Product pages may need refresh rules. Tenant-specific experiences need boundaries. Pricing, permissions, account state, and billing workflows need careful handling.

Caching should not start with “can we make this faster?”

It should start with “can this safely stay the same?”

Readiness check:
Cache strategy needs freshness ownership before broad rollout.

4. Customer-path monitoring

Provider status is useful, but it is not the full picture.

A provider may be available while customers in a specific region experience latency, packet loss, routing issues, or degraded workflow completion.

Teams should monitor more than infrastructure health.

They should monitor customer paths:

  • login,
  • dashboard load,
  • file upload,
  • search,
  • report generation,
  • API response,
  • AI workflow,
  • checkout,
  • support submission,
  • and any workflow tied to revenue or trust.

Global averages can hide local issues. A product can look healthy in aggregate while one customer region feels slow.

This is especially important when the company serves users across cities, countries, networks, or enterprise environments.

Readiness check:
Monitor the workflow from the places customers use it.

5. Fallback planning

Cloud readiness needs a plan for degraded paths.

Not every slowdown should produce the same user experience.

Some workflows can be queued. Some can show cached information. Some can switch to a lighter path. Some can retry in the background. Some need a clear status message. Some should pause until the system can respond safely.

Fallback is not an excuse to hide problems.

It is a way to keep customers guided when the ideal path is degraded.

A good fallback plan answers:

  • What can be delayed?
  • What can be queued?
  • What can be simplified?
  • What can use an alternate path?
  • What needs a human checkpoint?
  • What should the user see?
  • Who decides when fallback mode starts?

Readiness check:
The product should have a next path when the best path slows down.

6. Ownership map

Most cloud issues become harder when ownership is unclear.

Someone may own the cloud account. Someone else may own the product. Another person may own support. Finance may review cost. Engineering may manage incident response. Product may decide customer impact.

That can work if the handoffs are clear.

It becomes messy when nobody knows who owns the next step.

A cloud ownership map should answer:

  • Who owns cost attribution?
  • Who owns capacity reservations?
  • Who owns cache refresh?
  • Who owns regional monitoring?
  • Who owns fallback activation?
  • Who communicates customer impact?
  • Who reviews the incident afterward?

This does not need to become bureaucracy.

It needs enough clarity that the team does not discover ownership during an incident.

Readiness check:
A cloud decision is not ready until the handoff is named.

A practical cloud readiness scorecard

Use this as a lightweight review before a major cloud decision, AI workload, cache rollout, scaling plan, or infrastructure change.

Cost attribution

Can we explain which product, project, or workload benefits from discounts, credits, or shared infrastructure?

Score:

  • 0: Total bill only
  • 1: Usage is visible
  • 2: Usage and savings are attributed
  • 3: Ownership and renewal decisions are defined

Capacity timing

Can we match reserved capacity to prepared work?

Score:

  • 0: Capacity is booked before the workload is clear
  • 1: Workload is named
  • 2: Window and prerequisites are defined
  • 3: Utilization target and fallback are defined

Cache freshness

Can we reuse responses without confusing customers?

Score:

  • 0: No freshness rules
  • 1: Cacheable paths are identified
  • 2: Change triggers and TTLs are defined
  • 3: Refresh ownership and measurement are defined

Customer-path monitoring

Can we see whether customers can complete important workflows?

Score:

  • 0: Provider status only
  • 1: Infrastructure metrics
  • 2: Workflow checks
  • 3: Regional workflow checks and alerting

Fallback planning

Can the product guide customers when the preferred path is degraded?

Score:

  • 0: No fallback
  • 1: Manual response only
  • 2: Basic fallback for important workflows
  • 3: Fallback behavior, trigger, and owner are defined

Ownership map

Can the team name who owns each handoff?

Score:

  • 0: Ownership unclear
  • 1: Owners known informally
  • 2: Owners documented for key workflows
  • 3: Owners, triggers, and review process are documented

A scorecard like this is not meant to create ceremony.

It helps teams see which cloud decision is technically possible but operationally weak.

When to use this checklist

Use it before:

  • buying or renewing cloud commitments,
  • reserving AI compute capacity,
  • adding caching to customer-facing paths,
  • expanding to another region,
  • launching a new product workflow,
  • changing CDN or routing behavior,
  • introducing a high-volume AI workload,
  • or reviewing an incident that affected customer experience.

The checklist is most useful when cloud cost, performance, and product experience are starting to overlap.

That is where many SaaS teams begin to feel cloud complexity.

The useful takeaway

Cloud readiness is not only about provider choice, instance type, or uptime.

It is about whether the team can explain cost, use capacity well, keep data fresh, monitor customer paths, prepare fallback, and name ownership.

A cloud system becomes more dependable when the team can answer:

  • What does this cost?
  • Who benefits?
  • What must stay fresh?
  • Where do customers feel impact?
  • What happens when the preferred path slows down?
  • Who owns the handoff?

That is the difference between cloud infrastructure that works and cloud infrastructure that supports the product clearly.

Sources

  • Google Cloud: Share resource-based CUDs across projects
  • AWS: Amazon EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML pricing
  • Cloudflare Blog: Your Worker can now have its own cache in front of it
  • Google Cloud Service Health: India network traffic incident
  • Google Cloud: Personalized Service Health

But founders and product teams usually need a different view:

  • Can customers complete the workflow?
  • Can we explain the bill?
  • Can we reuse work safely?
  • Can we use reserved capacity well?
  • Can we see regional impact?
  • Can we respond when the preferred path slows down?

A cloud system can look technically sound while still creating cost confusion, slow customer paths, stale responses, or capacity waste.

That is why a readiness checklist should cover both infrastructure and product consequences.

The 6-check cloud readiness framework

1. Cost attribution

Cloud savings are useful only when the company can explain who benefited.

Shared discounts, committed use discounts, savings plans, credits, reservations, and platform-level infrastructure can reduce the total bill. But if those savings are pooled across projects, products, or workloads, the reporting model needs to stay clear.

A SaaS team should be able to answer:

  • Which workload created the usage?
  • Which product received the discount benefit?
  • Which project absorbed uncovered usage?
  • Which team owns renewal or adjustment?
  • Does the dashboard show allocated savings, or only total spend?

This matters because product decisions depend on interpretable numbers.

If an AI workload appears cheaper, the team needs to know whether it became more efficient or was covered by shared discount capacity.

If a product line looks profitable, the team needs to know whether infrastructure costs are being allocated in a way that matches usage.

Readiness check:
Cloud savings need attribution, not only a lower total.

2. Capacity timing

Reserved capacity can be valuable when work is prepared for the window.

It can become expensive when the team books capacity before the workload is ready.

This matters most for AI and ML workloads, batch processing, evaluation runs, high-volume migrations, and compute-heavy jobs that need specific capacity during a specific period.

Before reserving capacity, check:

  • What job is the capacity supporting?
  • When does the work need to run?
  • What must be ready before the window starts?
  • What utilization would justify the spend?
  • What happens if the workload slips?
  • Who owns the capacity decision?
  • How will success be measured?

The question is not only whether the compute is affordable.

The question is whether the work can use the window well.

Readiness check:
Capacity planning should follow workload readiness, not only pricing pressure.

3. Cache freshness

Caching can reduce latency and repeated compute work.

But caching also creates a freshness question.

A response that is safe to reuse can improve performance and cost. A response that should have changed can damage trust.

Before caching a product path, define:

  • What response is being cached?
  • Who sees that response?
  • What makes it change?
  • How long can it be reused?
  • Who owns purge or refresh?
  • What should never be cached?
  • What will be measured besides speed?

Public documentation pages may be safe to cache. Product pages may need refresh rules. Tenant-specific experiences need boundaries. Pricing, permissions, account state, and billing workflows need careful handling.

Caching should not start with “can we make this faster?”

It should start with “can this safely stay the same?”

Readiness check:
Cache strategy needs freshness ownership before broad rollout.

4. Customer-path monitoring

Provider status is useful, but it is not the full picture.

A provider may be available while customers in a specific region experience latency, packet loss, routing issues, or degraded workflow completion.

Teams should monitor more than infrastructure health.

They should monitor customer paths:

  • login,
  • dashboard load,
  • file upload,
  • search,
  • report generation,
  • API response,
  • AI workflow,
  • checkout,
  • support submission,
  • and any workflow tied to revenue or trust.

Global averages can hide local issues. A product can look healthy in aggregate while one customer region feels slow.

This is especially important when the company serves users across cities, countries, networks, or enterprise environments.

Readiness check:
Monitor the workflow from the places customers use it.

5. Fallback planning

Cloud readiness needs a plan for degraded paths.

Not every slowdown should produce the same user experience.

Some workflows can be queued. Some can show cached information. Some can switch to a lighter path. Some can retry in the background. Some need a clear status message. Some should pause until the system can respond safely.

Fallback is not an excuse to hide problems.

It is a way to keep customers guided when the ideal path is degraded.

A good fallback plan answers:

  • What can be delayed?
  • What can be queued?
  • What can be simplified?
  • What can use an alternate path?
  • What needs a human checkpoint?
  • What should the user see?
  • Who decides when fallback mode starts?

Readiness check:
The product should have a next path when the best path slows down.

6. Ownership map

Most cloud issues become harder when ownership is unclear.

Someone may own the cloud account. Someone else may own the product. Another person may own support. Finance may review cost. Engineering may manage incident response. Product may decide customer impact.

That can work if the handoffs are clear.

It becomes messy when nobody knows who owns the next step.

A cloud ownership map should answer:

  • Who owns cost attribution?
  • Who owns capacity reservations?
  • Who owns cache refresh?
  • Who owns regional monitoring?
  • Who owns fallback activation?
  • Who communicates customer impact?
  • Who reviews the incident afterward?

This does not need to become bureaucracy.

It needs enough clarity that the team does not discover ownership during an incident.

Readiness check:
A cloud decision is not ready until the handoff is named.

A practical cloud readiness scorecard

Use this as a lightweight review before a major cloud decision, AI workload, cache rollout, scaling plan, or infrastructure change.

Cost attribution

Can we explain which product, project, or workload benefits from discounts, credits, or shared infrastructure?

Score:

  • 0: Total bill only
  • 1: Usage is visible
  • 2: Usage and savings are attributed
  • 3: Ownership and renewal decisions are defined

Capacity timing

Can we match reserved capacity to prepared work?

Score:

  • 0: Capacity is booked before the workload is clear
  • 1: Workload is named
  • 2: Window and prerequisites are defined
  • 3: Utilization target and fallback are defined

Cache freshness

Can we reuse responses without confusing customers?

Score:

  • 0: No freshness rules
  • 1: Cacheable paths are identified
  • 2: Change triggers and TTLs are defined
  • 3: Refresh ownership and measurement are defined

Customer-path monitoring

Can we see whether customers can complete important workflows?

Score:

  • 0: Provider status only
  • 1: Infrastructure metrics
  • 2: Workflow checks
  • 3: Regional workflow checks and alerting

Fallback planning

Can the product guide customers when the preferred path is degraded?

Score:

  • 0: No fallback
  • 1: Manual response only
  • 2: Basic fallback for important workflows
  • 3: Fallback behavior, trigger, and owner are defined

Ownership map

Can the team name who owns each handoff?

Score:

  • 0: Ownership unclear
  • 1: Owners known informally
  • 2: Owners documented for key workflows
  • 3: Owners, triggers, and review process are documented

A scorecard like this is not meant to create ceremony.

It helps teams see which cloud decision is technically possible but operationally weak.

When to use this checklist

Use it before:

  • buying or renewing cloud commitments,
  • reserving AI compute capacity,
  • adding caching to customer-facing paths,
  • expanding to another region,
  • launching a new product workflow,
  • changing CDN or routing behavior,
  • introducing a high-volume AI workload,
  • or reviewing an incident that affected customer experience.

The checklist is most useful when cloud cost, performance, and product experience are starting to overlap.

That is where many SaaS teams begin to feel cloud complexity.

The useful takeaway

Cloud readiness is not only about provider choice, instance type, or uptime.

It is about whether the team can explain cost, use capacity well, keep data fresh, monitor customer paths, prepare fallback, and name ownership.

A cloud system becomes more dependable when the team can answer:

  • What does this cost?
  • Who benefits?
  • What must stay fresh?
  • Where do customers feel impact?
  • What happens when the preferred path slows down?
  • Who owns the handoff?

That is the difference between cloud infrastructure that works and cloud infrastructure that supports the product clearly.

Sources

  • Google Cloud: Share resource-based CUDs across projects
  • AWS: Amazon EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML pricing
  • Cloudflare Blog: Your Worker can now have its own cache in front of it
  • Google Cloud Service Health: India network traffic incident
  • Google Cloud: Personalized Service Health

fullArticle:
Cloud problems rarely start as one obvious failure.

-> Sometimes the bill improves, but nobody can explain which product benefited.

-> Sometimes GPUs are reserved, but the workload is not ready for the window.

-> Sometimes caching makes the product faster, but nobody owns refresh.

-> Sometimes the provider looks available, but users in one region still feel slow paths.

These are not separate problems. They point to the same operating question:

Is the cloud setup ready for customer usage, cost pressure, and changing product needs?

It is not a replacement for cloud architecture reviews, incident response plans, FinOps tooling, or provider documentation. It is a practical field checklist for SaaS teams that need to connect cloud decisions to product outcomes.
